vulnerability.title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the kernel.kptr_restrict kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict= To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: kernel.kptr_restrict =
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of kernel.kptr_restrict from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*kernel.kptr_restrict.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "kernel.kptr_restrict" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done # # Set sysctl config file which to sa

triage_decision.reason: The finding modifies a sysctl parameter to restrict kernel pointer exposure; the change can be applied persistently via /etc/sysctl.d without reboot and does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystems. It follows standard STIG hardening patterns for kernel hardening and poses minimal risk of lockout.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Potential reduction in kernel debugging information; None that would cause service outage or lockout

review_feedback: Review+QA approved but the verification scan failed. Try a different approach.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly comments out existing kernel.kptr_restrict entries and adds a dedicated drop-in with the restrictive value, but it could be improved with validation and safer file creation.
concerns: Potential impact on debugging and diagnostic tools that read kernel pointers
Risk of boot-time failures if the generated sysctl file contains syntax errors
Absence of checks to preserve existing values or verify current setting
Need to ensure correct ordering of sysctl files to guarantee the value takes precedence
suggested_improvements: Create the drop-in with atomic write (e.g., use a temporary file and mv) to avoid incomplete files
Verify the current kernel.kptr_restrict value before modifying files
Add a sanity check after sysctl -p to confirm the intended value is active
Consider documenting the change in change-management to coordinate with operations
